elements.sent-2411-001: The attention mechanism within the transformer architecture enables the model to weigh and combine tokens based on their relevance to the query.
elements.sent-2411-002: While self-attention has enjoyed major success, it notably treats all queries $q$ in the same way by applying the mapping $V^\top\text{softmax (Kq)$, where $V,K$ are the value and key embeddings respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we argue that this uniform treatment hinders the ability to control contextual \xuechen{sparsity and relevance.}
elements.sent-2411-004: As a solution, we introduce the ``Selective Self-Attention'' (SSA) layer that augments the softmax nonlinearity with a principled temperature scaling strategy.
elements.sent-2411-005: By controlling temperature, SSA adapts the contextual \xuechen{sparsity of the attention map to the query embedding and its position in the context window.}
elements.sent-2411-006: Through theory and experiments, we demonstrate that this alleviates attention dilution, aids the optimization process, and enhances the model's ability to control softmax spikiness of individual queries.
elements.sent-2411-007: We also incorporate temperature scaling for value embeddings and show that it boosts the model's ability to suppress irrelevant/noisy tokens.
elements.sent-2411-008: Notably, SSA is a lightweight method which introduces less than 0.5\% new parameters through a weight-sharing strategy and can be fine-tuned on existing LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-009: Extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that SSA-equipped models achieve a noticeable and consistent accuracy improvement on language modeling benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Attention is a pivotal mechanism in modern machine learning that allows the model to focus on and retrieve different parts of the data, enhancing its ability to capture contextual relationships across time and space.
elements.sent-2411-011: While it was originally developed for NLP tasks through the transformer architecture, it has enjoyed widespread success in other domains such as computer vision, sequence modeling, and reinforcement learning \cite{vaswani2017attention,raffel2020exploring,brown2020language,chowdhery2023palm,sanh2021multitask .}
elements.sent-2411-012: The canonical self-attention mechanism is a sequence-to-sequence map that outputs $\X\rightarrow\sft{\Qb\Kb^\top}\Vb$ where $\sft{\cdot}$ denotes the row-wise softmax nonlinearity and $\Qb$, $\Kb$, $\Vb$ are the query, key, and value embeddings obtained through linear projections of the input sequence $\X$.
elements.sent-2411-013: Through this process, for each query, the model creates a query-dependent composition of the input context.
elements.sent-2411-014: Importantly, the model has to accomplish two objectives: namely, capturing semantic similarity between tokens and also adjusting the contextual sparsity.
elements.sent-2411-015: Here, semantic similarity can be quantified through the angle between key-query embeddings and the contextual \xuechen{sparsity through the spikiness of the attention map.}
elements.sent-2411-016: While the importance of the former is clear, the latter is equally important given the fact that attention maps tend to be sparse in practice \cite{child2019generating,tarzanagh2023maxmargin,sahiner2022unraveling,chen2021scatterbrain .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In this paper, we argue that these two objectives can be at odds and, as a result, the self-attention layer may struggle to achieve both objectives simultaneously due to its relatively inflexible parameterization.
elements.sent-2411-018: To address this issue, we propose the Selective Self-Attention (SSA) layer that aims to decouple semantic similarity from contextual \xuechen{sparsity .}
elements.sent-2411-019: SSA relies on a principled application of temperature-scaling (TS) to query and value embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-020: For instance, given query embedding $\qb$, rather than computing $\sft{\Kb\qb}$, SSA computes $\sft{\tau(\qb)\cdot\Kb\qb}$ where $\tau(\qb)$ is the learnable inverse-temperature.
elements.sent-2411-021: Intuitively, this allows for better control of the context window because $\tau(\qb)$ can control contextual \xuechen{sparsity while the projection matrices $\W_k,\W_q$ can fully focus on controlling semantic similarity.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Figure \ref{fig:text_heat shows an example of the learned token temperatures when training the Pythia model with SSA.}
elements.sent-2411-023: In summary, we make the following theoretical and empirical contributions:.

elements.sent-2411-025: TS particularly helps when attention maps exhibit large variations in \xuechen{spikiness across different queries.}
elements.sent-2411-026: Real and synthetic experiments corroborate that TS enables spikier/sharper attention maps and mitigates attention dilution.
elements.sent-2411-028: We formalize the benefit of TS on value embeddings through a denoising perspective.
elements.sent-2411-029: Namely, we describe a denoising task where the linear value projection fails to filter the noisy tokens, and demonstrate how nonlinear scaling can boost denoising capability.
elements.sent-2411-030: We incorporate a term that adjusts the query-temperature according to the position in the context window.
elements.sent-2411-031: We show that this term can mitigate the dilution of attention scores caused by the increasing context length.
